Fundamentals of Electrochemistry

Section 14-2 Galvanic Cells
Section 14-3 Standard Potentials
Section 14-4 Nernst Equation
Section 14-5 E° and the Equilibrium Constant
Section 14-6 Biochemists Use E° ′

Electrochemistry (1 of 2)
Branch of analytical chemistry that uses electrical measurements of chemical systems for analytical purposes
For example, measuring dopamine concentrations in single cells (Chapter 1)

Electrochemistry (2 of 2)
Desirable attributes of electrochemistry for analytical purposes:
Relatively inexpensive equipment
Potential for miniaturization
Ability to measure some complex samples with little sample preparation

Redox Reactions (1 of 2)
Electrochemical measurements are based upon electrochemical reactions. Electrochemical reactions are commonly called redox reactions .
A redox reaction involves transfer of electrons from one reagent to another reagent.

Redox reactions involve electron transfer
The oxidizing agent , also called the oxidant , takes electrons from the reducing agent. In this process, the reducing agent is oxidized .
The reducing agent , also called the reductant , gives electrons to the oxidizing agent. In this process, the oxidizing agent is reduced .

Redox reactions (2 of 2)
Fe 3+ is the oxidizing agent because it takes an electron from V 2+ .
V 2+ is the reducing agent because it gives an electron to Fe 3+ .
As the reaction proceeds from left to right:
V 2+ is oxidized   (V 2+ → V 3+ + e − )
Fe 3+ is reduced (Fe 3+ + e − → Fe 2+ )

The electrochemical cell isolates the electrons
Michael Faraday (1791–1867) and other early scientists discovered that it was useful to separate the reducing and oxidizing agents and then to connect them with an appropriate electrical circuit that enabled the redox reaction to proceed. This type of apparatus is called an electrochemical cell .
An electrochemical cell can be readily connected to instruments that measure the electric current and potential associated with the redox reaction.
Michael Faraday

Electric Charge
Electrical charge is a measurable property of the electrons that are transferred in a redox reaction.
Electric charge ( q ) is measured in units of coulombs (C).
A single electron or proton has a charge of 1.602  10 −19 C.
The Faraday constant ( F ) gives the magnitude of the charge on a mole of electrons:
𝐹 = (1.602 × 10 −19 C) (6.022 × 10 23 mol −1 ) = 9.649 × 10 4 C/mol
Michael Faraday

Calculating the total charge of an ion
Relation between charge and moles:
𝑞 = 𝑛 ∙ (mol) ∙ 𝐹
q = charge (coulombs)
n = unit charges per molecule (dimensionless)
( mol ) = moles (mole)
F = Faraday constant (coulombs per mole)
For example, the total charge carried by one mole of Fe 3+ ions is
𝑞 = (3) (1 mol) (9.649 × 10 4 C/mol) = 2.89 × 10 5 C

Electric Current
Electric current is the quantity of charge flowing each second through a circuit.
The unit of current is the ampere , abbreviated A .
One ampere represents the flow of one coulomb of charge per second past a point in a circuit.

Example: Relating Current to Rate of Reaction (1 of 4)
Suppose that electrons are forced into a platinum wire immersed in a solution containing Sn 4+ (Figure 14-1), which is reduced to Sn 2+ at a constant rate of 4.24 mmol/h. How much current passes through the solution?
Figure 14-1

Example: Relating Current to Rate of Reaction (2 of 4)
Solution
Two electrons are required to reduce one Sn 4+ ion:
Sn 4+ + 2e − → Sn 2+
Electrons flow at a rate of (2 mmol e − / mmol Sn 4+ ) (4.24 mmol Sn 4+ /h) = 8.48 mmol e − /h, which corresponds to

Voltage, Work, and Free Energy
Any electric charge creates an electric potential .
Electric potentials of opposite sign are attractive.
Positive and negative charges attract each other.
Electric potentials of the same sign are repulsive.
Positive charges repel other positive charges; negative charges repel other negative charges.
The electric potential difference, E , between two points is the work required or which can be done when a unit charge moves from one point to the other.
Potential difference is measured in units of volts (V) .

Figure 14-2: The Water Hose Analogy
Current
Potential Difference

Relation between work, voltage, and charge
The energy harvested from a spontaneous redox can be used to do work.
Or work can be done to store energy by recharging a redox system.
1 V = 1 J/C
q = the total amount of charge transferred in the redox reaction
E = the potential difference through which the charge moves

Sign conventions for heat and work
Heat ( q ) flow from the surroundings to the system is positive.
Heat ( q ) flow from the system to the surroundings is negative.
Work ( w ) done on a system is positive.
Work ( w ) done on the surroundings is negative.
[Speaker Notes] Answer: 10.4 V

Example: Electrical Work (1 of 4)
How much work can be done by 2.4 mmol of electrons moving from a region with a potential of 0 V to a region with a potential of 0.27 V (or between any two points differing in potential by +0.27 V)? Negative electrons are attracted toward more positive potential, so they can do work as they move.
Example: Electrical Work (2 of 4)

Calculating Δ G : Gibbs Free Energy of Reaction
The Gibbs free energy change for the reaction of an electrochemical cell represents the maximum work that could be extracted from one mole of a redox reaction assuming perfect efficiency (no heat losses).
Δ G = Gibbs free energy change in joules (J)
E = potential difference in volts (V)
F = Faraday constant (C/mol)
n = unit charges per molecule
A negative Δ G corresponds to a spontaneous reaction as written.
A positive Δ G corresponds to a nonspontaneous reaction as written.
[Speaker Notes] Answer: 10.4 V

Ohm’s Law
Ohm's law states that current, I , is directly proportional to the potential difference, E , across a circuit and inversely proportional to the resistance , R , of the circuit.
The greater the potential difference ( E ), the more current ( I ) that will flow.
The greater the resistance ( R ), the less current ( I ) that will flow.

Power
Power , P , is the work done per unit time.
The SI unit of power is the watt (W) .
1 W = 1  J / s
𝑃 = 𝐸 ∙ 𝐼 = (𝐼 ∙ 𝑅) ∙ 𝐼 = 𝐼 2 ∙ 𝑅
A cell that delivers 1 A of current through a potential difference of 1 V will generate 1 W of power.

Example: Using Ohm’s Law (1 of 3)
In the circuit in Figure 14-4, the battery generates a potential difference of 3.0 V, and the resistor has a resistance of 100 Ω. The wire connecting the battery and the resistor has negligible resistance. Find the current and power delivered by the battery.

A Galvanic Cell
A galvanic cell (also called a voltaic cell ) uses a spontaneous redox reaction to generate electricity.
A battery is a galvanic cell.
In a galvanic cell (battery), the oxidizing and reducing agents are organized in a manner that allows the user to control when current flows and when it does not.
A replica of Allessandro Volta’s original battery (invented in 1799) is shown. If the top is connected to the bottom by a wire, current will flow.

A Cell in Action (1 of 2)
Figure 14-5 shows a galvanic cell with two electrodes suspended in a solution of CdCl 2
At the Cd electrode, Cd metal is oxidized to Cd 2+ ( aq ).
The electrode at which oxidation occurs is the anode.
Electrons flow through the external circuit from the Cd electrode to the Ag electrode.
At the Ag electrode, Ag + from AgCl is reduced to Ag metal.
The electrode at which reduction occurs is the cathode.

Half-Reactions and Net Reactions
The net reaction is composed of a reduction and an oxidation, each of which is called a half-reaction .
The two half-reactions are written with equal numbers of electrons before adding to obtain the net reaction .
Reduction:
Oxidation:
Net reaction:
Figure 14-5

A Cell in Action (2 of 2)
The potentiometer in the circuit measures the difference in electric potential (voltage) between the two metal electrodes.
𝐸 measured = 𝐸 + − 𝐸 −
E + is the potential of the electrode attached to the positive terminal.
E − is the potential of the electrode attached to the negative terminal.

Example: Voltage Produced by a Chemical Reaction (1 of 3)

Divided cell with a salt bridge (1 of 2)
A salt bridge is a U-shaped tube filled with a gel containing KNO 3 or other electrolyte not involved in the reaction.
Separate the reactants into two half-cells connected with a salt bridge:
The Ag + cannot diffuse to the wrong electrode.
Oxidation and reduction happen at the proper electrode.
Electrons flow through the circuit, enabling the galvanic cell to work.
To prevent charge buildup in each half-cell:
K + migrates from the salt bridge into the cathode compartment.
migrates from the salt bridge into the anode compartment.
Figure 14-7

Line Notation for Galvanic cells
Figure 14-7
The design of a galvanic cell can be summarized using line notation.
A single line represents a phase boundary (for example, between a solid electrode and a liquid solution).
A double line represents a salt bridge.
Electrodes are shown at extreme left- and right-hand sides.
Anode | components of anode half-cell || components of cathode half-cell| cathode
Cd(𝑠) | Cd(NO 3 ) 3 (𝑎𝑞) || AgNO 3 (𝑎𝑞) | Ag(𝑠)
